speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] The date is November 7th, 1996. We're with Darrio Gabbai, interviewer Carol Stulberg, in the city of Los Angeles, the state of California, United States of America, language spoken English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Carol Stulberg, S-T-U-L-B-E-R-G. I have the privilege of interviewing Mr. Dario Gabbai on November the 7th, 1996 in Los Angeles, California, United States of America. The language will be English. Good morning, Mr. Gabbai.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please start by giving us your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name is David Darrio Gabbai.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell it for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-A-B-B-A-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the name you were given at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names, any Hebrew or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's-- that's the full name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell us where and when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Thessaloniki, or Salonika, Greece at that time, in September 2, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today I'm 74 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your parents, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, my father was Victor Gabbai and my mother was Rosa Beraha Gabbai.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were your parents originally from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Originally, we started from the Spanish Inquisition, 1492, from Spain, going across the continents. And my father and mother were born in Smyrna, Turkey. And then going back, they went to Salonika at that time. And then became-- Salonika became Greece, you know, from Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father worked all his life as a typographer for the Greek newspaper called which means new-- new truth. And he worked there since we went back to-- to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother ever work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother never worked. Used to stay home, take care of the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many kids were there in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were three brothers, and I was the middle one. Jack or Jacob was the older one, and Samuel was the youngest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me when they were born so we know the differences in your ages?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my brother, the older one, was born in 1913. I was born in 1922. And Samuel was in 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other siblings at all that you knew about, sisters or other brothers that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was one system that was die at birth, you know, in the '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, please, what your life was like as a boy in Salonika.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were Italian citizens living in Greece. And we used to have a permit to work there, but were permanent residents of Greece. And we had to go every year to just renew our permit to work. Mostly I went to an Italian school, and-- where we-- we had to learn about four languages. And it was a beautiful school. But at that time was the era of the Fascist government in Italy. And I finished in 1938, the high school of the Italian school. And I was ready to go to an Italian university, study-- mostly I would like to be a doctor. But unfortunately at that time in 1938, Hitler and Mussolini got together and they didn't let anymore Jewish Italians go to the Italian universities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get quite that far in your story, let me take you back a little bit to when you were younger and you were just a young boy. What kind of a neighborhood did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We live in-- mostly in a Jewish neighborhood. The-- we lived in a quarter called 151 in Salonika. Actually, the Jewish community bought all that section from-- actually, they were barracks of the Italian army from the First World War. And when the-- when the First World War finished in 1919, that was left over, and the Jewish community bought it and we were living there practically for very little money, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in this neighborhood where you lived, did you have a lot of close relatives living by, maybe grandparents, or uncles and aunts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- my grandparents on my-- on my mother's side living-- were living with us, in the same-- in the same-- actually in the same house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names were-- her name was Allegra, you know, and his name was Bohor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their last name were Beraha.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of activities did you used to like to do as a young boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a lot on activities in the Italian school, actually a lot of athletic activities there. And we-- I was also going every year-- the school took us for two, three months in the summer to Italy, free you know. And at that time, I used to play in a band with a clarinet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy playing the clarinet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was very much. It was beautiful. We used to go to-- they used to take us to Como, di Lago Como, and then to Cortina d'Ampezzo, you know, and then the last month we used to be in Rome. We used to go about for seven days by boat from-- from Salonika to Italy. And from there, it was a beautiful three months during the summer, because it didn't cost us anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your friends? What kind of friends did you have in-- in Salonika?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have-- you know, we're enough Jewish Italians-- a Jewish Italian colony in Salonika. And lots of them were well-to-do people. And I had quite a few friends. And one of the-- the friends is-- was Daniel Bennahmias that later on we spent most of our lives together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community like in Salonika? Was-- I wasn't involved too much as a Jew. But the Jewish community of Salonika were-- before the war, the Jewish population in Salonika was close to 100,000 in 200,000 population. It was 50%. And I remember that on Saturdays especially the port of Salonika was closed because none of the Jewish people would work. And downtown was closed every Saturday. There-- there were a lot of synagogues and a lot of different other institutions that were helping the poor people of-- the Jewish poor people in Salonika. What about Jewish observance in your family? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother was pretty good in observing the Jewish tradition. And I and my father were more liberals at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your grandparents? Were they fairly--

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents were, you know, pretty-- pretty good. They-- my grandparents and my mother's family were pretty religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to synagogue with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very seldom I went to the synagogue with my family. But my mother I know used to go every-- every Saturday and especially during the Jewish holidays. The Jewish holidays, we all went together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the Jewish holidays like in Greece? How were they celebrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very-- they-- they celebrate every-- every holiday, you know. There were so many-- so many Jewish institutions, so many things and schools and elementary schools and Jewish schools, you know. So it was very-- pretty good Jewish life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Jewish holidays in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My home, we used to-- to have our Jewish holidays. My mother always saw that we were there for the Jewish holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite was always Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these were the high holidays what everybody used to get dressed and, you know, like the old-- old time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was a Passover at your house, let's say? What kind of food did your mother prepare?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prepare the-- the food that the Sephardic used to-- to have. And we were-- we were enough-- we used to be maybe 8, 10 people. You'd get together. And we had a very, very interesting dinner during the Jewish-- you know, the Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- tell us about it. Describe us-- describe it for us. Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, they-- they-- the Sephardics made very, very good stuff. We used to have the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chicken and rice and beans, you know, that the part of it, and lots of fruits and vegetables. Everything the-- no matter what, how poor we were, you know, always during the holidays, the table was always full of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship like between you and your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- with my older brother, I-- I wasn't too close, you know. We were different about nine years, eight, nine years, so-- we were together in many occasions, but we weren't as close as we should be. My younger brother was-- was too young at that time. But we were-- a family, very loving family always from the start. Parents always they-- they were very good parents. I had very good parents. Always they loved us and they gave us as much as they could do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of relationship did you have with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, very, very close relationship. Father and mother very, very close. I used to go with my father-- I remember before he goes to work, we used to go and fish practically two, three times a week. We used to walk in the morning, 5:30 in the morning for about 30 minutes to get to the sea from our place where we lived. And you just go.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a big-- at that time, an Italian Jew had the biggest, the [NON-ENGLISH] they called it, [NON-ENGLISH]. They were making the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do they call it? There were meals. There were-- so-- and they had a long-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what they call it? A long-- to go to sea-- they had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] You mean like a--

speaker: interviewee
text: A platform, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you mean like a pier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A pier. They had a pier going all the way there. And we used to go to the pier and stay there until the time from 5:30, 6 o'clock until 8:30, when my father used to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of fish did you catch?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to catch, they call it-- in Italian they call it branzino, which is striped bass.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would happen to the fish? Would your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: We used-- yeah. We used to take it. In the evening, we used to have a full meal at home. You know, my mother was waiting all the time to have a fresh-- fresh fish for dinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who used to have to clean the fish?

speaker: interviewee
text: She has to clean the fish. She had to do everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, as a young boy, did you belong to any youth organizations or any kind of Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: We use to belong-- at that time, we were Italians Jews. We used to belong to an Italian youth organization at school. This is the organization that used to take us to Italy every-- every year for free for vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I had a bar mitzvah. I don't remember exactly, but I had a bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so you have no recollection that you could tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the house that you lived in. What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were at least-- the house, we had two bedrooms, for-- one bedroom for us, one bedroom for our grandparents. And on the other side were another two bedrooms for another family. That was the Modiano family that lived with us for many, many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they also a Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Jewish Italians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- in Salonika, was there any kind of differentiation between the Italian Jews and the Greek Jews, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Italian Jews, we always going to Italian affairs, you know, at that time. That's whey when some of the-- there were Italian Jews that were very powerful. And they were also involved in all the Jewish affairs. But we were-- but the Italians had a lot of affairs at that time, you know. They had dances. And so we used to go there, because we were part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We speak a couple of languages. We-- we spoke Greek, Italian, and Ladino.

speaker: interviewer
text: Among yourselves, when you were together with your immediate family, what would you speak usually?

speaker: interviewee
text: We speak mostly Greek and Ladino. That's the two languages we-- we used to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were with your friends, what languages did you speak with your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were speaking Greek, actually, with our friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that all of your friends were Jewish, or did you have Gentile friends as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- we had quite a few Gentile friends, you know, but mostly were Jewish. Our friends were mostly Jewish, because at one point, when I was in the Italian school, I used to belong also to the-- to Jewish organizations, you know, the-- the Jabotinsky actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do while you were that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were always-- at that time, you know, Israel, you know, was Palestine. And we were always talking about-- we had somebody there that-- to talk us about-- about Jabotinsky, the big capo of the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend any kind of a Hebrew school or a cheder when were a young boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the synagogue that your family went to, the Sephardic synagogue, could you describe it for us? Tell us what it was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember too much. I know that the synagogue was across from-- from a-- a river, you know. When it was-- we had a lot of the rain, that became a river. And across from that was a synagogue. It was very close to-- to the house, in walking distance. I don't-- I don't remember what it looks like right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the school that you went to you said before was an Italian school. How many years did you attend that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended from-- from elementary school. Was about the five and four-- nine years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you finished school what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I finished school, once I couldn't go to any university-- and my father arranged that I work for the same newspaper he was doing, at that time just translating from Italian to Greek or French to Greek at that time, for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that, approximately? How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1938, '38 to '40. In-- I was 16, 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something. While you were growing up in Salonika in Greece, did you ever experience any kind of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. In 1933, was quite a bit of anti-Semitism, especially in the Jewish neighborhoods. There were two, three neighborhoods. And then, at that time, was the-- they call it the Three Es were an organization that was against-- against the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what that stood for, the letters? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Three Epsilon they call it in Greek was an organization that was against the Jews at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what type of anti-Semitic activities were going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they would try to burn the neighborhoods or do-- you know, put fires. That's-- that's why my uncle that lived in a different neighborhood, you know, decide to take his family and go-- at that time, go into Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around what year was that again?

speaker: interviewer
text: So it-- it kind of corresponded to when Hitler was coming into power in Germany. Of course, you were pretty young at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was there any talk within your home between your family of what was happening or what they thought?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Sure. We were all aware what was happening. But I-- I think since we were Italian citizens, they wouldn't-- they were-- they knew where we were, so we were pretty safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of books and newspapers did you have in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had all kinds. You know, first of all, we had a lot of Italian-- Italian books and newspapers, and especially Greek newspapers. My father was working, bringing home all the time the newspaper he was working on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like to read as a young boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I always-- I like-- I don't read books enough. But I read a lot of magazines and newspapers. I always want-- want to find out what the world is all about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as you got a little older and things started to progress in Europe, let's say around 1938, do you remember reading about things that were going on and what you thought?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We were-- we were reading-- no, especially in 1939 when the war started, you know, with Germany, you know, declaring war to Poland. At that time, you know, then the Italians also, in-- a year later in 1940, declared war to Greece. So what happened, during that time, that the Greeks took our family. They left my father and my little brother home. And me, my brother, and my father, they took us in a camp, a concentration camp in southern Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Argos, Greece. That-- that was the camp. But since all the Italians-- some of the Italians were very well known and very good friends of the king of Greece. After a few months, they decided that they will pay their own way and bring them to close to Athens, in Marousi in a small hotel where they were-- the-- the rich people will pay for everything. And we stay there until the end of the war, for another six months. We were just-- just closed in a-- in a hotel, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went to this hotel in Athens, when they came and took you away from your home--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what did they tell you? Why were they taking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because we were Italian citizens. And they thought once Italy declare war, we will be with Italy instead of with Greece. We were Italian citizens. [INAUDIBLE] you know, we were Italians.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this camp like in Argos?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Argos, it wasn't bad. You know, it was-- after I went to-- to Poland, you know, that was pretty good, and especially when we moved us to a hotel. We just stayed there until the end doing nothing really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in this camp in Argos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 1941, for a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was approximately--

speaker: interviewee
text: Both, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: One year. And so after you left this camp and you went back to Athens--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- no. We went-- I went back to Salonika in 1941. At that time, the Germans game in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before going back to Salonika, let me just clarify something, after the camp you went to this hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Marousi, which is close to Athens. And from there, when we finish, we went back to our homes in-- in Salonika.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Few months, five, six months, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the whole time you were there, what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We were-- nothing to do, really. We were just reading and going-- was-- they had a big outside of the hotel, so we stayed around there. It wasn't bad. It was very good at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have people guarding you or making sure that you stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. Yeah. There was a guard, but just on the one garden. They were very close to the king, most of the Italian Jews, to the king of Greece. And they had very good relationships, so I think he arranged it to be-- once they were-- it cost nothing to the Greek government.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after returning to Salonika, what were things like when you came back? When I came back, the newspaper didn't exist anymore. The Germans came in. They closed the newspaper. And I-- I was trying to-- to find a job, actually for over-- over a year. Another Danish cousin, the father had a mill to grind up-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- what they call it? To make bread, you know, just the-- the wheat.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were coming from the-- around-- around Salonika, the people they had wheat, you know. They had to mill it. So they came down to us and I worked for a year, you know, all night long actually, doing that kind of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was from 1941 to 1943, when the Germans started surrounding the-- the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that period of time, 1941 to 1943, generally how would you describe the life for the Jews in Greece?

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- at one point, was OK, the first year. But then I think the Germans started asking a lot of questions, going to the Jewish community and find out who they are. You know, they started to have, you know, I think in 1942 the-- what was the-- the head in Germany? The-- what was the meeting they had for the Final Solution? Wannsee. Wannsee. The Wannsee.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after that, you know, they-- they start just going after the Jews of Salonika. There were lots of them. So this is how-- but we went-- we left in 1943. The Italian government put us in an Italian, they call it [ITALIAN], in an Italian-- the-- they were coming to Salonika, railroads from Italy, and they took us to the Italian part of Greece. Half of it was the Germans, and from Larissa down to southern Greece, the Italians occupied then. And they put us in a school where we stayed there until I was caught by the Germans in the synagogue in March, 1944. Before we get to that, while you were still in Salonika, before you left, were the Germans that were there treating the Greek Jews differently than the Italian Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- they didn't-- they didn't do us-- We had in our door, we had from Italian-- from the Italian consulate a piece of paper that we're Italians. So the Jews, they didn't touch us. They didn't do anything, even though that they were complaining to the Italian authorities. The Italian government said that they shouldn't touch us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what types of things did you see happening to the Greek Jews around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were- they were doing-- you know, little by little, they were putting in ghettos. And then they-- I didn't see too much. I-- I saw when they start taking them, you know, because I had a lot of friends of mine, you know, they went from Salonika. Friends from school and-- and regular young friends of mine that they were taken. You know, they started transports. I was already-- when they start taking them I was already in Athens.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before you left for Athens, would you say that your life pretty much went along as it did before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, went along. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there problems with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish people and the Germans that were occupying the area as far as collaboration was involved? Did you witness anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I-- when I was in Athens, you know-- but there were always Jewish traitors around the whole thing. But you know, they saved themselves for a while, but at the end they all perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the time came for you to leave Salonika and go to Athens, when was this exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was in-- in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm going to stop the tape now. We're going to change the tape.
